title: Immutable reference
instruction:
Pass `&value` to borrow without moving. The function uses a reference `&String`.

Run this.
successMessage: &s borrows s. After length() returns, s is still valid because the function never owned it.

title: Many immutable borrows
instruction:
Any number of immutable references can coexist — they all promise not to mutate.

Run this.
successMessage: Three simultaneous immutable borrows are safe. They all read the same memory; no conflicts possible.

title: Mutable reference
instruction:
`&mut` borrows mutably — you can modify through the reference.

The value must be declared `mut`. Create the reference with `&mut`.

Run this.
successMessage: &mut s grants mutation rights. The function changes s through the reference. After the call, s reflects the change.

title: One mutable borrow at a time
instruction:
Only one mutable reference can exist at a time. This prevents data races.

Run this — it will fail. Read the error.
hint: Remove one of the mutable references.

codeChecks.message: You cannot have two mutable borrows at once. Remove r2 or restructure the code.

successMessage: This rule eliminates data races at compile time. If two threads both held &mut, they could corrupt data simultaneously.

title: Cannot mix mutable and immutable
instruction:
Active immutable borrows prevent mutable borrows — they promise the data will not change.

Run this — it will fail.
hint: Use r1 and r2 before creating the mutable borrow r3.

codeChecks.message: r1 is still active when r2 is created. Use r1 first, then create the mutable borrow.

successMessage: Immutable borrows guarantee no mutation. A mutable borrow would break that guarantee. Once r1 is no longer used, r2 is valid.

title: Non-Lexical Lifetimes (NLL)
instruction:
Borrows end at their last use, not at end of scope. This makes the rules more flexible.

Run this — it works!
successMessage: NLL: the immutable borrow r1 ends at its last use. After that, the mutable borrow r2 is safe. Modern Rust is much more ergonomic because of NLL.

title: No dangling references
instruction:
Rust guarantees references always point to valid data.

This code will fail — Rust prevents returning a reference to local data.

Run it to see the error.
hint: Return the String directly instead of a reference to it.
successMessage: This works because we return the String (transferring ownership). If we returned &s, Rust would reject it — s would be dropped before the caller uses the reference.

title: Borrowing summary
instruction:
The rules:
1. Any number of immutable borrows, OR exactly one mutable borrow
2. References are always valid (no dangling references)

Run this.
expectedOutput: 6 words: 'to be or not to be'
successMessage: speech is borrowed by summarise (as &str — string slice). After the call, speech is still valid. Borrowing is the everyday Rust pattern for working with data.
